messages.content: Build a user-profile loader in Effect-TS v3 two ways: one using pipe with Effect.flatMap, one using Effect.gen. Same signature, same behaviour — fetch a user by id, then fetch their team, and return both.
What's the Effect v3 way to translate a three-step pipe + Effect.flatMap chain into Effect.gen? Show a minimal example.
Explain briefly when Effect.flatMap is still preferable to Effect.gen, then show a working typed example that makes the distinction clear.

Refactor this Effect v3 flatMap chain into Effect.gen. Keep the same signature and typed error channel: `const run = (id: string) => pipe(fetchRow(id), Effect.flatMap((row) => pipe(parse(row.body), Effect.flatMap((payload) => publish(payload)))));`

Write an Effect v3 program that reads a config string, parses an integer, and multiplies by 2. Constraints: provide both the pipe+Effect.map form and the Effect.gen form side-by-side, share the same error type, no any.
Build an Effect v3 checkout pipeline — validate cart, compute total, charge card — and show the equivalent Effect.gen and pipe + Effect.flatMap forms.
Explain briefly when to reach for applicative (ap) vs monad (chain) in fp-ts 2.x Either, then show a typed example that uses each correctly.
Build an fp-ts 2.x Option example that contrasts O.map (functor), O.ap (applicative), and O.chain (monad) on the same three optional inputs.
What's the fp-ts 2.x way to pick between Apply.sequenceT and E.chain when combining three Either values?

Write an fp-ts 2.x function that parses two form fields independently and returns Either<string, {name:string; age:number}>. Constraints: use applicative ap (not chain), accumulate nothing, strict types, no any.
Build a typed fp-ts 2.x example showing that O.ap cannot encode a dependent second step — then show the equivalent O.chain that can.
What's the cleanest fp-ts 2.x way to lift a two-argument function over Option using ap? Show a minimal example.
Explain what Kleisli composition is for Effect v3 (f: A -> Effect<B>, g: B -> Effect<C>), then show a working typed example that composes two Kleisli arrows into one A -> Effect<C>.
Build a Kleisli pipeline in fp-ts 2.x TaskEither: parse a string into a URL, fetch the body, then parse JSON. Compose the three arrows using TE.chain.
Add a Kleisli identity arrow idK and demonstrate both left- and right-identity laws against this composeK helper: `const composeK = <A,B,C,E1,E2>(f,g) => (a) => pipe(f(a), Effect.flatMap(g))`
Write an Effect v3 program that composes three Kleisli arrows (validate -> normalise -> persist). Constraints: use pipe + Effect.flatMap, typed errors accumulate in the E channel, no any, no gen.
Fix this broken Kleisli composition in Effect v3 — the author used Effect.map where Effect.flatMap is needed: `pipe(parseId(raw), Effect.map(loadUser))`
Build a reusable Kleisli.compose3 helper in Effect v3 that takes three arrows A -> Effect<B>, B -> Effect<C>, C -> Effect<D> and returns A -> Effect<D>. Preserve typed errors via union.
Build a hand-rolled Lens<S, A> type in fp-ts 2.x style and use it to get and set User.address.city immutably, without any external optics library.
What's the fp-ts 2.x way to update a nested field immutably through a hand-rolled Lens without rewriting the rest of the record? Show a minimal example.
Refactor this hand-written nested spread into a composed Lens-based update: `const shift = (s: Shape, dx: number): Shape => ({ ...s, origin: { ...s.origin, x: s.origin.x + dx } })`
Add a withTheme: (theme: 'light' | 'dark') => (u: User) => User updater to this lens module: `const settings = prop<User>()('settings')`

Write an fp-ts 2.x style module that exposes a three-level Lens into Order.customer.contact.email plus a helper lowercaseEmail. Constraints: three-level nesting, use a hand-rolled compose, strict types, no any.
Build a fp-ts 2.x Optional-style optic over an array's head element (getOption + set) using fp-ts/Option, and show both existence check and modification through it.
Build an Effect v3 batch processor that parses a list of raw strings into numbers and uses Effect.partition to split the successes and the failures.
Explain briefly how Effect.partition differs from Effect.all in Effect v3, then show a typed example that uploads a list of files, keeps the successful upload ids, and reports every failure reason.
Build an Effect v3 pipeline that geocodes a list of addresses concurrently using Effect.partition so one failing address doesn't abort the rest.
What's the Effect v3 way to know which inputs survived and which failed after a partition? Show a minimal example that logs per-failure.
Build a fp-ts 2.x ReaderTaskEither program that reads a Config from the environment, fetches a row by id, and returns Either<DbError, Row>. Wire the config via ask.
What's the fp-ts 2.x way to combine two ReaderTaskEither values that share the same environment? Show a minimal example.
Explain briefly what ReaderTaskEither<R, E, A> represents in fp-ts 2.x and why it's the combined monad of choice for async code with typed deps and typed errors, then show a typed example.

Fix this fp-ts 2.x ReaderTaskEither so that it reads baseUrl from the environment via RTE.asks instead of closing over a hardcoded string, and still handles throws via tryCatch: `export const health = pipe(TE.tryCatch(async () => ({ ok: (await fetch('http://localhost/health')).ok }), ...))`
Add a retry layer to this RTE program so that transient errors are retried twice before surfacing: `export const load = (id: string): RTE.ReaderTaskEither<Deps, E, Row> => ...`
Build an Effect v3 program that records the current timestamp via Clock, sleeps 50ms, and records a second timestamp — returning both.
What's the Effect v3 way to delay a program by a typed Duration? Show a minimal example.
Explain briefly why Clock is a service in Effect v3 rather than a direct Date.now() call, then show a typed example that uses Clock.currentTimeMillis to stamp a log line.
Write an Effect v3 function runWithTimeout that races an effect against Effect.sleep(ms) and fails with a TimeoutError when the sleep wins. Constraints: typed tagged error, use Effect.race, no any.
Add a latency-measuring wrapper around this program using Clock in Effect v3: `declare const load: Effect.Effect<Row>`
